# File-mode differential cases.
#
# Format per case (one case per non-empty paragraph):
#   SETUP: <SQL fed to `sqlite3 fix.db` to build the fixture>
#   QUERY: <SQL fed to BOTH `sqlite3 fix.db` and `sqlite0 -file fix.db` for diff>
#
# Each blank-line-separated block is one case. Lines starting with `#` are
# ignored. SETUP runs once via the system sqlite3 CLI to materialise the
# .db file; QUERY then runs against both engines and the outputs must match
# byte-for-byte.

SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(a, b); INSERT INTO t VALUES (1, 'x'), (2, 'y'), (3, 'z');
QUERY: SELECT * FROM t

SETUP: CREATE TABLE n(x INTEGER); INSERT INTO n VALUES (10), (20), (30), (40);
QUERY: SELECT x FROM n WHERE x > 15

SETUP: CREATE TABLE p(name TEXT, age INTEGER); INSERT INTO p VALUES ('alice', 30), ('bob', 25), ('carol', 35);
QUERY: SELECT name FROM p WHERE age >= 30 ORDER BY name

SETUP: CREATE TABLE k(c); INSERT INTO k VALUES ('apple'), ('banana'), ('cherry');
QUERY: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM k

SETUP: CREATE TABLE pair(a, b); INSERT INTO pair VALUES (1, 2), (3, 4), (5, 6);
QUERY: SELECT a + b FROM pair

# Multi-page B-tree: 500 rows of moderate width force the table B-tree to
# split into multiple leaves (and likely an interior root). Exercises
# btree_walk.TableLeafWalker end-to-end on a real sqlite3-emitted tree.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE big(n INTEGER, label TEXT); WITH RECURSIVE c(i) AS (SELECT 1 UNION ALL SELECT i+1 FROM c WHERE i < 500) INSERT INTO big SELECT i, 'row-' || i FROM c;
QUERY: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM big

SETUP: CREATE TABLE big(n INTEGER, label TEXT); WITH RECURSIVE c(i) AS (SELECT 1 UNION ALL SELECT i+1 FROM c WHERE i < 500) INSERT INTO big SELECT i, 'row-' || i FROM c;
QUERY: SELECT n FROM big WHERE n = 1 OR n = 250 OR n = 500 ORDER BY n

# Two tables → JOIN through both BtreeCursor instances.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE a(id, v); CREATE TABLE b(id, w); INSERT INTO a VALUES (1, 'x'), (2, 'y'); INSERT INTO b VALUES (1, 100), (2, 200);
QUERY: SELECT a.v, b.w FROM a JOIN b ON a.id = b.id ORDER BY a.id

# Aggregate over a real-file table.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE g(grp TEXT, val INTEGER); INSERT INTO g VALUES ('a', 1), ('a', 2), ('b', 3), ('b', 4), ('b', 5);
QUERY: SELECT grp, SUM(val) FROM g GROUP BY grp ORDER BY grp

# NULL handling in record decode. Both engines walk a table B-tree in
# rowid (= insertion) order without an explicit ORDER BY, so the output
# matches without referencing the rowid pseudo-column (which sqlite0
# doesn't expose yet — Iter25.B.5 scope).
SETUP: CREATE TABLE n2(a, b); INSERT INTO n2 VALUES (1, NULL), (NULL, 2), (3, 4);
QUERY: SELECT a, b FROM n2

# Real numbers (8-byte IEEE 754 BE).
SETUP: CREATE TABLE r(x REAL); INSERT INTO r VALUES (1.5), (-2.25), (3.14159);
QUERY: SELECT x FROM r ORDER BY x

# BLOB column.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE bl(payload BLOB); INSERT INTO bl VALUES (x'deadbeef'), (x'00ff');
QUERY: SELECT length(payload) FROM bl

# Iter26.A.1 INSERT cases (write path). The harness clones the fixture,
# runs MUTATE through both engines on independent copies, then runs
# VERIFY against both and diffs. PRAGMA integrity_check on the
# sqlite0-mutated copy catches byte-format corruption that VERIFY alone
# might not surface.

# Single INSERT into an empty file-mode table.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(a, b)
MUTATE: INSERT INTO t VALUES (1, 'x')
VERIFY: SELECT * FROM t

# Multi-row INSERT in one statement.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(a, b)
MUTATE: INSERT INTO t VALUES (1, 'a'), (2, 'b'), (3, 'c')
VERIFY: SELECT a, b FROM t

# INSERT into a non-empty table — rowids must continue past max.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(a); INSERT INTO t VALUES (10), (20)
MUTATE: INSERT INTO t VALUES (30), (40)
VERIFY: SELECT a FROM t

# Mixed-type INSERT (NULL, REAL, TEXT on the same row). BLOB literals
# (`x'...'` syntax) aren't accepted by the sqlite0 parser yet — that's
# unrelated to the file-mode write path and tracked separately.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE m(i, f, t)
MUTATE: INSERT INTO m VALUES (NULL, 3.14, 'hi')
VERIFY: SELECT i, f, t FROM m

# Two separate INSERT statements in one sqlite0 invocation.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(x)
MUTATE: INSERT INTO t VALUES (1); INSERT INTO t VALUES (2)
VERIFY: SELECT x FROM t

# Iter26.A.2.a DELETE cases (file-mode rebuild-page).

# DELETE without WHERE — drop everything from a populated table.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(a); INSERT INTO t VALUES (1), (2), (3)
MUTATE: DELETE FROM t
VERIFY: SELECT count(*) FROM t

# DELETE WHERE matching a subset.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(a, b); INSERT INTO t VALUES (1, 'x'), (2, 'y'), (3, 'z')
MUTATE: DELETE FROM t WHERE a = 2
VERIFY: SELECT a, b FROM t

# DELETE WHERE matching no rows — table contents and on-disk bytes unchanged.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(a); INSERT INTO t VALUES (10), (20)
MUTATE: DELETE FROM t WHERE a = 999
VERIFY: SELECT a FROM t

# DELETE then re-SELECT after a mixed INSERT+DELETE batch.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(x); INSERT INTO t VALUES (1), (2), (3), (4)
MUTATE: DELETE FROM t WHERE x > 2
VERIFY: SELECT x FROM t

# Iter26.A.2.b UPDATE cases (file-mode rebuild-page).

# UPDATE without WHERE — rewrite every row.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(a, b); INSERT INTO t VALUES (1, 'x'), (2, 'y')
MUTATE: UPDATE t SET b = 'z'
VERIFY: SELECT a, b FROM t

# UPDATE WHERE matching one row, single column.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(a, b); INSERT INTO t VALUES (1, 'old'), (2, 'old'), (3, 'old')
MUTATE: UPDATE t SET b = 'new' WHERE a = 2
VERIFY: SELECT a, b FROM t

# UPDATE multiple columns at once.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE p(name TEXT, age INTEGER); INSERT INTO p VALUES ('alice', 30), ('bob', 25)
MUTATE: UPDATE p SET name = 'BOB', age = 26 WHERE name = 'bob'
VERIFY: SELECT name, age FROM p ORDER BY name

# UPDATE WHERE matches no rows — file unchanged.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(a); INSERT INTO t VALUES (1), (2)
MUTATE: UPDATE t SET a = 99 WHERE a = 999
VERIFY: SELECT a FROM t

# UPDATE growing the row's record (small int → larger int).
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(n INTEGER); INSERT INTO t VALUES (5), (10)
MUTATE: UPDATE t SET n = 1000000 WHERE n = 5
VERIFY: SELECT n FROM t

# UPDATE assignment-order: every RHS sees the OLD row. sqlite3 yields
# (11, 1) for (a + 10, a) because b's RHS reads the pre-update a.
# A naive in-place mutation between assignments would yield (11, 11).
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(a, b); INSERT INTO t VALUES (1, 0)
MUTATE: UPDATE t SET a = a + 10, b = a
VERIFY: SELECT a, b FROM t

# Iter26.A.3 file-mode CREATE TABLE cases. Both engines run the same
# MUTATE on independent file copies (each starting from the SETUP
# fixture); VERIFY then runs against both and the outputs must match.
# `PRAGMA integrity_check` on the sqlite0-mutated copy catches any
# byte-format divergence that VERIFY alone might not surface.
#
# Round-trip is skipped for these cases because VERIFY queries
# `sqlite_schema` directly, which sqlite0 doesn't yet expose as a
# user-queryable table (it only reads it internally during open).

# CREATE TABLE on an existing file, then SELECT confirms the schema
# is visible to sqlite3.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE seed(x)
MUTATE: CREATE TABLE u(a, b)
VERIFY: SELECT name FROM sqlite_schema WHERE type='table' ORDER BY name
SKIP_RT: 1

# CREATE + INSERT + SELECT round-trip through sqlite0; sqlite3 then
# reads the inserted rows.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE seed(x)
MUTATE: CREATE TABLE u(a, b); INSERT INTO u VALUES (1, 'hello'), (2, 'world')
VERIFY: SELECT a, b FROM u

# CREATE TABLE preserves column types in the stored sql column —
# sqlite3 reads the schema row's sql to drive its own typing.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE seed(x)
MUTATE: CREATE TABLE u(id INTEGER, name TEXT)
VERIFY: SELECT sql FROM sqlite_schema WHERE name='u'
SKIP_RT: 1

# Two CREATE TABLE statements in one batch — both must land and both
# must show up in sqlite_schema.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE seed(x)
MUTATE: CREATE TABLE a1(p); CREATE TABLE a2(q)
VERIFY: SELECT name FROM sqlite_schema WHERE type='table' AND name LIKE 'a%' ORDER BY name
SKIP_RT: 1

# Iter26.B.1 balance-deeper cases. Each MUTATE issues a single INSERT
# whose combined cell list overflows one leaf (~100 rows of ~50-byte
# text), forcing executeInsertFile down the balance-deeper path: the
# leaf root gets converted into an interior root pointing at two new
# leaf children, with the root_page number kept stable so the
# sqlite_schema entry remains valid. integrity_check on the
# sqlite0-mutated copy verifies the resulting B-tree is structurally
# valid for sqlite3, and the harness's sqlite0 round-trip read closes
# the loop (sqlite0 must re-read what it just wrote).

# Total row count after balance-deeper.

MUTATE: INSERT INTO t VALUES (1, 'padding-string-of-50-chars-aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa-001'),(2, 'padding-string-of-50-chars-aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa-002'),(3, MUTATE: UPDATE t SET s = 'all'
VERIFY: SELECT count(*) FROM t WHERE s = 'all'

# Iter26.C.1 read-side overflow chain. sqlite3 INSERTs a 6000-byte
# TEXT row (≥ X = 4049 on 4084 usable), which spills into one
# overflow chain page. sqlite0 must walk the chain on SELECT.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(s); INSERT INTO t VALUES (replace(hex(zeroblob(3000)), '0', 'a'))
QUERY: SELECT length(s), substr(s, 1, 5), substr(s, 5990, 10) FROM t

# Iter26.C.1: oversize row in a multi-row table — chain must be
# parsed alongside inline-only siblings.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(n, s); INSERT INTO t VALUES (1, 'short'),(2, replace(hex(zeroblob(3500)), '0', 'a')),(3, 'tail')
QUERY: SELECT n, length(s) FROM t ORDER BY n

# Iter26.C.2 INSERT-side overflow: a 6000-char string spills into one
# overflow chain page (P=6004 on 4084 usable → inline=487, spill=5517 →
# 2 chain pages). SETUP creates an empty table, MUTATE inserts the
# oversize row, VERIFY round-trips length/prefix/suffix through both
# engines + integrity_check.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(s)
MUTATE: INSERT INTO t VALUES ('')
VERIFY: SELECT length(s), substr(s, 1, 5), substr(s, 5990, 10) FROM t

# Iter26.C.2: two oversize rows. Each takes M=487 inline bytes plus a
# 4-byte chain head, so the leaf comfortably holds both — exercises
# the multi-cell case without dragging in needs_split.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(n, s)
MUTATE: INSERT INTO t VALUES (1, ''),(2, '')
VERIFY: SELECT n, length(s), substr(s, 1, 1) FROM t ORDER BY n

# Iter26.C.3 DELETE oversize row → chain enters freelist; integrity_check
# verifies no orphan pages.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(s); INSERT INTO t VALUES ('')
MUTATE: DELETE FROM t
VERIFY: SELECT count(*) FROM t

# Iter26.C.3 UPDATE shrinking oversize → fits inline. Old chain freed,
# new payload stays on the leaf.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(s); INSERT INTO t VALUES ('')
MUTATE: UPDATE t SET s = 'short'
VERIFY: SELECT s, length(s) FROM t

# Iter26.C.3 UPDATE growing inline → oversize. Allocates a fresh chain.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(s); INSERT INTO t VALUES ('short')
MUTATE: UPDATE t SET s = ''
VERIFY: SELECT length(s), substr(s, 1, 1), substr(s, 5995, 5) FROM t

# Iter26.C: multi-leaf table where one row has an overflow chain.
# sqlite3 SETUP balances ~50 rows across an interior root + 2 leaves
# (one row is oversize so its leaf carries an inline+chain cell).
# sqlite0 must walk the depth-1 tree on SELECT and round-trip the
# chain on a non-matching DELETE.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(n, s); INSERT INTO t VALUES (1, 'padding-row-001-of-50-aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa'),(2, 'padding-row-002-of-50-aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa'),(3, 'padding-row-003-of-50-aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa'),(4, 'padding-row-004-of-50-aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa'),(5, 'padding-row-005-of-50-aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa'),(6, 'padding-row-024-of-50-aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa'),(25, ''),(26, 'padding-row-026-of-50-aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa'),(27, 'padding-row-027-of-50-aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa'),(28, 'padding-row-028-of-50-aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa'),(29, 'padding-row-029-of-50-aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa'),(30, 'padding-row-030-of-50-aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa'),(31, 'padding-row-031-of-50-aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa'),(32, 'padding-row-032-of-50-aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa'),(33, 'padding-row-033-of-50-aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa'),(34, 'padding-row-034-of-50-aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa'),(35, 'padding-row-035-of-50-aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa'),(36, 'padding-row-036-of-50-aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa'),(37, 'padding-row-037-of-50-aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa'),(38, 'padding-row-038-of-50-aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa'),(39, 'padding-row-039-of-50-aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa'),(40, 'padding-row-040-of-50-aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa'),(41, 'padding-row-041-of-50-aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa'),(42, 'padding-row-042-of-50-aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa'),(43, 'padding-row-046-of-50-aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa'),(47, 'padding-row-047-of-50-aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa'),(48, 'padding-row-048-of-50-aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa'),(49, 'padding-row-049-of-50-aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa'),(50, 'padding-row-050-of-50-aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa')
QUERY: SELECT count(*), length(s) FROM t WHERE n = 25

# Iter26.B.3 depth-2 INSERT. SETUP creates a depth-2 tree (sqlite3
# balances ~600 wide rows into root-interior + multiple interior-children
# + ~600 leaves). MUTATE INSERTs one row, exercising the spine descend
# (root -> interior child -> leaf), leaf split (rightmost leaf is full),
# and bottom-up promote into the rightmost interior child (.fits path).
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(n, s); WITH RECURSIVE c(n) AS (SELECT 1 UNION ALL SELECT n+1 FROM c WHERE n<600) INSERT INTO t SELECT n, zeroblob(3000) FROM c
MUTATE: INSERT INTO t VALUES (601, 'tail')
VERIFY: SELECT count(*), max(n) FROM t

# Iter26.B.3 depth-2 UPDATE. Recursive collectLeafPages must walk
# every leaf under every interior child of the depth-2 tree.
# UPDATE re-encodes matched rows in place — neither leaf is left
# empty. (sqlite3 raises CORRUPT on empty leaves under non-root
# parents — the spec only allows empty leaves at the root. Proper
# underfull rebalance is a future iteration; DELETE that would
# wipe a leaf clean is therefore deliberately not exercised here.)
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(n, s); WITH RECURSIVE c(n) AS (SELECT 1 UNION ALL SELECT n+1 FROM c WHERE n<600) INSERT INTO t SELECT n, zeroblob(3000) FROM c
MUTATE: UPDATE t SET s = 'updated' WHERE n IN (1, 100, 300, 500, 600)
VERIFY: SELECT n, s FROM t WHERE s = 'updated' ORDER BY n

# Iter26.B.3 depth-2 read across all leaves. Tests the recursive
# btree_walk arm that enumerates leaves under each interior child.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(n, s); WITH RECURSIVE c(n) AS (SELECT 1 UNION ALL SELECT n+1 FROM c WHERE n<600) INSERT INTO t SELECT n, zeroblob(3000) FROM c
QUERY: SELECT count(*), min(n), max(n), length(s) FROM t WHERE n IN (1, 200, 400, 600)


# ---- Iter27.A: WAL read-side ----
# WAL_SETUP runs after SETUP under `.dbconfig no_ckpt_on_close on`,
# leaving the -wal sidecar populated when sqlite0 opens the file.
# sqlite0 must scan the WAL, populate its in-memory page index, and
# return WAL-resident page versions from getPage so SELECT matches
# sqlite3 (which sees the same WAL on its own subsequent open).

# Single INSERT inside WAL — page 2 (the table root) lives in WAL,
# never made it to main. sqlite0 SELECTing must read it from -wal.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(x);
WAL_SETUP: INSERT INTO t VALUES (42);
QUERY: SELECT * FROM t

# Multi-row INSERT in WAL — leaf page is rewritten in-WAL each row.
# Latest-version-wins inside the index because all writes commit.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(n, s);
WAL_SETUP: INSERT INTO t VALUES (1, 'one'), (2, 'two'), (3, 'three');
QUERY: SELECT n, s FROM t ORDER BY n

# CREATE TABLE inside WAL — sqlite_schema (page 1) gets a new
# leaf cell appended in WAL. Schema scan at open-time must read
# the WAL-resident page 1, otherwise the table is invisible.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE pre(x); INSERT INTO pre VALUES (1);
WAL_SETUP: CREATE TABLE post(y); INSERT INTO post VALUES (99);
QUERY: SELECT (SELECT count(*) FROM pre), (SELECT count(*) FROM post)

# UPDATE in WAL — same page rewritten with new bytes; the WAL frame
# overrides the original page even though both have the same page_no.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(n); INSERT INTO t VALUES (1), (2), (3);
WAL_SETUP: UPDATE t SET n = n + 100;
QUERY: SELECT n FROM t ORDER BY n

# DELETE in WAL — leaf rebuild in-WAL leaves a smaller page.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(n); INSERT INTO t VALUES (1), (2), (3), (4), (5);
WAL_SETUP: DELETE FROM t WHERE n % 2 = 0;
QUERY: SELECT count(*), min(n), max(n) FROM t

# Connection-died-mid-tx canary. The unCOMMITted INSERT (999) is not
# guaranteed to reach -wal — sqlite3's page cache may keep it in memory
# until commit forces a frame write. The tentative-then-promoted
# discard path is covered by the synthetic wal_recovery.zig unit test
# that controls bytes on disk directly. This case acts as an
# end-to-end regression canary: whichever way sqlite3 lays out the
# bytes, sqlite0 must agree on the visible rows.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(n); INSERT INTO t VALUES (1);
WAL_SETUP: INSERT INTO t VALUES (2); BEGIN; INSERT INTO t VALUES (999);
QUERY: SELECT n FROM t ORDER BY n

# Iter27.B.3 — sqlite0 mutates a WAL fixture. SETUP makes the table,
# WAL_SETUP populates one row inside WAL, MUTATE inserts a second row
# THROUGH sqlite0 (which appends a WAL frame + commit + fsync via the
# new write path), VERIFY checks both engines see both rows. Also
# closes the round-trip (sqlite0 reads back its own WAL writes) and
# runs PRAGMA integrity_check on the sqlite0-mutated copy. This is
# the discriminating test the advisor singled out: failure modes
# are interpretable.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(x INTEGER);
WAL_SETUP: INSERT INTO t VALUES(1);
MUTATE: INSERT INTO t VALUES(2);
VERIFY: SELECT * FROM t ORDER BY x

# Same as above but multiple rows + UPDATE — exercises restage of the
# same page across multiple writeFrame calls within one statement.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(n INTEGER, s TEXT);
WAL_SETUP: INSERT INTO t VALUES(1, 'one');
MUTATE: INSERT INTO t VALUES(2, 'two'); INSERT INTO t VALUES(3, 'three'); UPDATE t SET s = upper(s);
VERIFY: SELECT n, s FROM t ORDER BY n




# Iter27.B.3+ — staged-page LRU eviction regression. 10 rows × 100
# byte TEXT spans multiple leaf pages within one statement; getPage
# during the cascading allocate/split must reach back into
# `staged_frames` for pages already evicted from the 16-entry LRU.
# Without the staged_frames consult in pager.zig, this surfaces as
# IoError (newly-allocated page beyond EOF) or stale bytes from
# pre-tx main-file reads. Advisor-caught hole.
#
# Iter28 restored `n INTEGER PRIMARY KEY` here. The MUTATE supplies
# explicit IPK values 1..10 strictly greater than the WAL_SETUP-seeded
# rowid 0, so the monotonic-rightmost spine walker can take them
# verbatim. VERIFY's count/min/max validate that the read path
# substitutes the cell rowid for the IPK column on cold reopen.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(n INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, s TEXT);
WAL_SETUP: INSERT INTO t VALUES(0, 'seed');
MUTATE: INSERT INTO t(n,s) VALUES(1,'aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa'),(2,'bbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbb'),(3,'cccccccccccccccccccccccccccccccccccccccccccccccccccccccccccccccccccccccccccccccccccccccccccccccccc'),(4,'dddddddddddddddddddddddddddddddddddddddddddddddddddddddddddddddddddddddddddddddddddddddddddddddddd'),(5,'eeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeee'),(6,'ffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffff'),(7,'gggggggggggggggggggggggggggggggggggggggggggggggggggggggggggggggggggggggggggggggggggggggggggggggggg'),(8,'hhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhh'),(9,'iiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiiii'),(10,'jjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjjj');
VERIFY: SELECT count(*), min(n), max(n) FROM t

# Iter27.B.4 — WAL MUTATE coverage expansion. DELETE through WAL.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(n INTEGER, s TEXT);
WAL_SETUP: INSERT INTO t VALUES(1, 'a'), (2, 'b'), (3, 'c'), (4, 'd');
MUTATE: DELETE FROM t WHERE n > 2;
VERIFY: SELECT n, s FROM t ORDER BY n

# UPDATE-only through WAL — no row-count change, just page rewrite.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(n INTEGER, s TEXT);
WAL_SETUP: INSERT INTO t VALUES(1, 'old'), (2, 'old'), (3, 'old');
MUTATE: UPDATE t SET s = 'new' WHERE n = 2;
VERIFY: SELECT n, s FROM t ORDER BY n

# CREATE TABLE through WAL — sqlite_schema (page 1) gets a new cell
# appended; the new table's root page must be allocated through the
# WAL stage, not extend the main file.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE seed(x); INSERT INTO seed VALUES (1);
WAL_SETUP: INSERT INTO seed VALUES (2);
MUTATE: CREATE TABLE u(a, b); INSERT INTO u VALUES (10, 'x'), (20, 'y');
VERIFY: SELECT (SELECT count(*) FROM seed), (SELECT count(*) FROM u)

# DELETE-everything through WAL — leaf rebuild, freelist update, page-1
# header bump.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(a INTEGER);
WAL_SETUP: INSERT INTO t VALUES (1), (2), (3);
MUTATE: DELETE FROM t;
VERIFY: SELECT count(*) FROM t

# UPDATE expression through WAL — `a = a + 10` exercises the read-
# then-write path on the same page within one statement.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(a INTEGER, b INTEGER);
WAL_SETUP: INSERT INTO t VALUES (1, 100);
MUTATE: UPDATE t SET a = a + 10, b = a;
VERIFY: SELECT a, b FROM t

# Multiple statements per execute() — the per-statement commit hook
# in Database.execute fires twice. Each must produce a valid commit
# frame; recovery sees both.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(n INTEGER);
WAL_SETUP: INSERT INTO t VALUES (1);
MUTATE: INSERT INTO t VALUES (2); INSERT INTO t VALUES (3);
VERIFY: SELECT n FROM t ORDER BY n

# Iter27.B.5 — multi-session reopen. MUTATE writes through sqlite0 once,
# the process exits (closing both fds), MUTATE2 opens the same file in
# a brand-new sqlite0 invocation. The second session must inherit our
# OWN previously-written WAL chain via WalWriter.fromExistingState
# (chain seed, salts, next_frame_offset all recovered from the on-disk
# frames). Without correct inheritance, MUTATE2 either rewrites a
# committed frame (chain seed off) or appends with a wrong checksum
# (sqlite3's reader rejects the new tail). Advisor-flagged as the
# load-bearing pre-C correctness check; the existing single-process
# MUTATE only exercises fromExistingState against a sqlite3-written WAL.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(n INTEGER);
WAL_SETUP: INSERT INTO t VALUES (1);
MUTATE: INSERT INTO t VALUES (2);
MUTATE2: INSERT INTO t VALUES (3); UPDATE t SET n = n * 10 WHERE n = 1;
VERIFY: SELECT n FROM t ORDER BY n

# Same shape but starting from a sqlite3-fresh WAL_SETUP-less DB so
# the first sqlite0 session is `wal_writer.create` and the second is
# `fromExistingState` against ONLY our own bytes — discriminates "we
# can chain after sqlite3 wrote" (covered by other cases) from "we
# can chain after we ourselves wrote".
SETUP: CREATE TABLE u(a INTEGER, b TEXT);
WAL_SETUP: SELECT 1;
MUTATE: INSERT INTO u VALUES (1, 'first');
MUTATE2: INSERT INTO u VALUES (2, 'second'); INSERT INTO u VALUES (3, 'third');
VERIFY: SELECT a, b FROM u ORDER BY a

# Iter27.C — `PRAGMA wal_checkpoint` returns three integers
# `(busy, log_pages, ckpt_pages)`. Empty/uninitialised WAL on a
# delete-mode DB → `0|-1|-1`. Active WAL → `0|N|N` where N is the
# physical frame count. TRUNCATE form resets the WAL → `0|0|0`.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(n INTEGER);
QUERY: PRAGMA wal_checkpoint

# Active WAL with one INSERT — PASSIVE leaves the file alone, returns
# 0|1|1.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(n INTEGER);
WAL_SETUP: INSERT INTO t VALUES (1);
QUERY: PRAGMA wal_checkpoint

# Multiple frames + PASSIVE: returns 0|2|2.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(n INTEGER);
WAL_SETUP: INSERT INTO t VALUES (1); INSERT INTO t VALUES (2);
QUERY: PRAGMA wal_checkpoint(PASSIVE)

# TRUNCATE on a populated WAL: returns 0|0|0 (file shrunk back to
# header). The post-state -wal sidecar is just 32 bytes.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(n INTEGER);
WAL_SETUP: INSERT INTO t VALUES (1); INSERT INTO t VALUES (2);
QUERY: PRAGMA wal_checkpoint(TRUNCATE)

# Post-checkpoint readability — the discriminating test the advisor
# flagged. PRAGMA reset state must leave the database queryable in
# the same process: cache entries match the freshly-truncated main
# file, WalState reset to empty, future reads pull from main. A bug
# in cache invalidation, ftruncate sizing, or the new-WAL re-attach
# would surface here as missing rows or stale bytes.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(n);
WAL_SETUP: INSERT INTO t VALUES (1), (2);
QUERY: PRAGMA wal_checkpoint(TRUNCATE); SELECT n FROM t ORDER BY n

# Same shape with PASSIVE — WAL stays intact, post-checkpoint reads
# go through the unchanged WAL index. Catches PASSIVE-specific
# regressions (e.g. accidentally clearing state.index).
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(n);
WAL_SETUP: INSERT INTO t VALUES (1), (2);
QUERY: PRAGMA wal_checkpoint(PASSIVE); SELECT n FROM t ORDER BY n

# Iter28 — INTEGER PRIMARY KEY column-rowid aliasing. The advisor's
# minimal discriminating fixture: explicit IPK value 5 must surface
# back through SELECT as `5|x`, not `1|x` (auto-rowid would yield
# the latter on a fresh table).
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(n INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, s);
MUTATE: INSERT INTO t VALUES(5,'x');
VERIFY: SELECT n,s FROM t

# Cold-reopen round-trip: MUTATE adds an IPK table to an existing
# fixture; VERIFY's third pass (sqlite0 round-trip line in the
# harness) re-opens the file fresh, re-parses sqlite_schema, and the
# IPK metadata must survive that loop. WHERE n=5 also exercises the
# rowid substitution in the engine_dml_file WHERE evaluation path.
# `bootstrap` exists only to ensure the .db file is created — sqlite3
# refuses to materialise the file on a bare SELECT.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE bootstrap(x);
MUTATE: CREATE TABLE t(n INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, s); INSERT INTO t VALUES(5,'x'),(7,'y');
VERIFY: SELECT n,s FROM t WHERE n=5

# Omitted-IPK INSERT auto-assigns the rowid like sqlite3. Three rows,
# explicit max=10, then omitted INSERT picks 11. Cell records store
# NULL for the IPK column; SELECT must read back the rowid.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, val);
MUTATE: INSERT INTO t(id,val) VALUES(10,'a'); INSERT INTO t(val) VALUES('b');
VERIFY: SELECT id,val FROM t ORDER BY id

# WHERE on IPK column is the path most users hit. The substitution
# must be live during DELETE's WHERE eval too: with three rows
# (rowids 1,2,3) DELETE WHERE id=2 must drop exactly one row.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, val);
MUTATE: INSERT INTO t VALUES(1,'a'),(2,'b'),(3,'c'); DELETE FROM t WHERE id=2;
VERIFY: SELECT id,val FROM t ORDER BY id

# Aggregate over IPK exercises the read path through Cursor +
# materializeRows. count/min/max all reference the IPK column.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, val);
MUTATE: INSERT INTO t VALUES(3,'c'),(7,'g'),(10,'j');
VERIFY: SELECT count(*),min(id),max(id) FROM t

# Iter27.E — BEGIN + COMMIT batches multiple INSERTs into a single
# WAL commit frame. The discriminating bit is that the per-statement
# auto-commit hook in Database.execute is suppressed while
# in_transaction is set; without that gating, each INSERT would emit
# its own commit frame and the eventual frame count diverges from
# sqlite3 (which also batches under one BEGIN). VERIFY just confirms
# the batched rows are visible after COMMIT.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(n INTEGER);
WAL_SETUP: SELECT 1;
MUTATE: BEGIN; INSERT INTO t VALUES (1); INSERT INTO t VALUES (2); INSERT INTO t VALUES (3); COMMIT;
VERIFY: SELECT n FROM t ORDER BY n

# BEGIN + ROLLBACK — staged frames discarded, file byte-identical
# (modulo WAL bytes) to the pre-BEGIN state. SELECT in VERIFY only
# sees the WAL_SETUP-seeded row. The cache-invalidation path
# (pager_wal.rollback evicting cached pages the staged INSERT
# write-through populated) is the load-bearing piece — without it
# the next read would surface the rolled-back bytes from cache.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(n INTEGER);
WAL_SETUP: INSERT INTO t VALUES(99);
MUTATE: BEGIN; INSERT INTO t VALUES (1); INSERT INTO t VALUES (2); ROLLBACK;
VERIFY: SELECT n FROM t

# COMMIT after a no-op tx (BEGIN; SELECT; COMMIT) is a no-op for the
# pager but should still toggle in_transaction cleanly. The SELECT
# inside the tx sees pre-tx state because nothing was staged.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(n INTEGER);
WAL_SETUP: INSERT INTO t VALUES(7);
MUTATE: BEGIN; SELECT n FROM t; COMMIT;
VERIFY: SELECT n FROM t

# Mixed DML inside a BEGIN block — INSERT + DELETE in one tx.
# COMMIT flushes both as one batch.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(n INTEGER);
WAL_SETUP: INSERT INTO t VALUES(1),(2),(3);
MUTATE: BEGIN; INSERT INTO t VALUES (10); DELETE FROM t WHERE n = 2; COMMIT;
VERIFY: SELECT n FROM t ORDER BY n

# PRAGMA journal_mode (read-only, Iter27.E) — reports the mode the
# file opened in. WAL_SETUP forces the file into WAL mode, so the
# PRAGMA returns "wal" (matches sqlite3 byte-for-byte).
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(n);
WAL_SETUP: SELECT 1;
QUERY: PRAGMA journal_mode

# Non-WAL file: PRAGMA reports "delete".
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(n);
QUERY: PRAGMA journal_mode

# ROLLBACK cache-invalidation discriminating fixture (Iter27.E).
# `BYTE-EQ + ok` for the BEGIN+ROLLBACK case above only proves the
# WAL stage was discarded — it cannot catch the in-process cache
# bug where pager_wal.writeFrame's write-through left post-mutation
# bytes in `pager.cache`. The MUTATE here reads in the SAME
# sqlite0/sqlite3 invocation, so the SELECT after ROLLBACK forces
# the cache path: if `evictCachedPage` were missing we'd surface 3
# (the rolled-back rows leaked from cache); sqlite3 returns 1.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(n INTEGER);
WAL_SETUP: INSERT INTO t VALUES(99);
MUTATE: BEGIN; INSERT INTO t VALUES (1); INSERT INTO t VALUES (2); ROLLBACK; SELECT count(*) FROM t;
VERIFY: SELECT n FROM t

# Iter27.F — SAVEPOINT/RELEASE/ROLLBACK TO. Partial-rollback inside
# an explicit BEGIN: the rolled-back INSERTs (2,3) vanish, the
# pre-savepoint INSERT (1) and post-rollback INSERT (99) survive.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(n INTEGER);
WAL_SETUP: SELECT 1;
MUTATE: BEGIN; INSERT INTO t VALUES (1); SAVEPOINT s1; INSERT INTO t VALUES (2); INSERT INTO t VALUES (3); ROLLBACK TO s1; INSERT INTO t VALUES (99); RELEASE s1; COMMIT;
VERIFY: SELECT n FROM t ORDER BY n

# Implicit-tx SAVEPOINT: no surrounding BEGIN. SAVEPOINT opens an
# implicit tx that the matching RELEASE commits — the loop hook in
# Database.execute flushes the staged batch when in_transaction
# transitions back to false.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(n INTEGER);
WAL_SETUP: SELECT 1;
MUTATE: SAVEPOINT s1; INSERT INTO t VALUES (1); INSERT INTO t VALUES (2); RELEASE s1;
VERIFY: SELECT n FROM t ORDER BY n

# Same-page across savepoint boundary (discriminating snapshot test).
# All 5 INSERTs land on the same leaf page. After ROLLBACK TO s1,
# the staged page snapshot must reflect the "1 row" version, NOT
# the "5 rows" version that writeFrame's in-place memcpy left in
# staged_frames[0].data. Without per-savepoint snapshot the SELECT
# would surface count=5 (silent corruption); sqlite3 returns 1.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(n INTEGER);
WAL_SETUP: SELECT 1;
MUTATE: BEGIN; INSERT INTO t VALUES (1); SAVEPOINT s1; INSERT INTO t VALUES (2); INSERT INTO t VALUES (3); INSERT INTO t VALUES (4); INSERT INTO t VALUES (5); ROLLBACK TO s1; COMMIT; SELECT count(*),sum(n) FROM t;
VERIFY: SELECT n FROM t

# Nested SAVEPOINTs inside BEGIN. ROLLBACK TO outer drops the
# inner savepoint stack frame too. Then a new INSERT post-rollback
# survives the COMMIT.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(n INTEGER);
WAL_SETUP: SELECT 1;
MUTATE: BEGIN; INSERT INTO t VALUES (1); SAVEPOINT a; INSERT INTO t VALUES (2); SAVEPOINT b; INSERT INTO t VALUES (3); ROLLBACK TO a; INSERT INTO t VALUES (99); RELEASE a; COMMIT;
VERIFY: SELECT n FROM t ORDER BY n

# Full ROLLBACK should drop the entire savepoint stack — RELEASE
# afterwards would error if any frame leaked. Verifies popAllSavepoints
# fires on .rollback regardless of whether the savepoint is still in
# the named stack.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(n INTEGER);
WAL_SETUP: INSERT INTO t VALUES (10);
MUTATE: BEGIN; SAVEPOINT s1; INSERT INTO t VALUES (1); ROLLBACK;
VERIFY: SELECT n FROM t

# RELEASE inside an explicit BEGIN must NOT drop in_transaction (the
# savepoint isn't `is_implicit_tx`). Without the guard the loop hook
# would flush staged frames mid-tx, then the COMMIT line below would
# either commit a half-built batch or raise. With the guard, the
# post-RELEASE INSERT(1) accumulates and the COMMIT flushes the whole
# batch as one frame.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(n INTEGER);
WAL_SETUP: SELECT 1;
MUTATE: BEGIN; SAVEPOINT s1; RELEASE s1; INSERT INTO t VALUES (1); COMMIT;
VERIFY: SELECT n FROM t

# Iter29.A — sqlite_schema queryable. The synthetic registration in
# `schema.loadFromPager` aliases page 1's sqlite_schema btree so
# `SELECT ... FROM sqlite_schema` routes through BtreeCursor at
# root_page=1. Returns the same 5 columns sqlite3 exposes: type,
# name, tbl_name, rootpage, sql.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE alpha(a, b); CREATE TABLE beta(x INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, y);
QUERY: SELECT type, name FROM sqlite_schema ORDER BY name

# sqlite_master alias (the legacy name; sqlite3 still accepts both).
SETUP: CREATE TABLE alpha(a, b); CREATE TABLE beta(x);
QUERY: SELECT name FROM sqlite_master WHERE type='table' ORDER BY name

# SELECT * on sqlite_schema — exposes all 5 columns including the raw
# CREATE TABLE source text in the sql column. Discriminating: byte-
# identical sql strings only land if our schema scanner preserved the
# original source through the page 1 record (i.e. no CREATE statement
# normalisation happened on the way in).
SETUP: CREATE TABLE alpha(a, b);
QUERY: SELECT * FROM sqlite_schema

# WHERE filter against type column.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE alpha(a); CREATE TABLE beta(b);
QUERY: SELECT name FROM sqlite_schema WHERE type='table' ORDER BY name

# count(*) on sqlite_schema — table count.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE alpha(a); CREATE TABLE beta(b); CREATE TABLE gamma(c);
QUERY: SELECT count(*) FROM sqlite_schema

# WAL-mode sqlite_schema query. Verifies that the synthetic
# registration in `schema.loadFromPager` works regardless of journal
# mode — page 1's btree is read through BtreeCursor either way, and
# WAL frames for newer schema rows must surface in the cursor too.
# Discriminates: any code that special-cased rollback-mode at the
# scanner layer would diverge here.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE alpha(a, b);
WAL_SETUP: CREATE TABLE delta(d INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, e);
QUERY: SELECT type, name FROM sqlite_schema ORDER BY name

# Iter29.B — NOT NULL constraint enforcement. Schema metadata is
# re-parsed from the sqlite_schema sql column on open; the file-mode
# INSERT path checks `not_null[col]` after IPK auto-assignment and
# rejects NULL with `Error.ConstraintNotNull`. Each fixture pairs the
# same INSERT shape against both engines; on rejection, run_query_case
# collapses non-zero exit to "<error>" on both sides and the case
# passes.
#
# Omitted column → NOT NULL violated (column 'a' is NOT NULL, INSERT
# provides only 'b'). sqlite3 returns SQLITE_CONSTRAINT (19), sqlite0
# returns ConstraintNotNull (exit 1) — both non-zero, both surface as
# "<error>" in the harness.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(a NOT NULL, b);
QUERY: INSERT INTO t(b) VALUES(1)

# Explicit NULL into NOT NULL column.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(a NOT NULL, b);
QUERY: INSERT INTO t VALUES(NULL, 1)

# UPDATE setting NOT NULL column to NULL (explicit literal).
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(a NOT NULL, b); INSERT INTO t VALUES(1, 2);
QUERY: UPDATE t SET a = NULL WHERE b = 2

# INTEGER PRIMARY KEY NOT NULL + omitted IPK → succeeds (auto-rowid
# satisfies the constraint per sqlite3's "IPK NULL → rowid" rule).
# Discriminating: with a naive "after-rebuild check" the IPK column
# would appear NULL in the encoded record body and mistakenly trip
# the constraint.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(a INTEGER PRIMARY KEY NOT NULL, b NOT NULL);
MUTATE: INSERT INTO t(b) VALUES('hello');
VERIFY: SELECT a, b FROM t

# Multi-row INSERT where the second row violates NOT NULL — sqlite3
# rolls the entire statement back per stmt-level atomicity, so neither
# row should land. With our pre-pass `prepareNewCells` validating
# every row before any pwrite, the first row's overflow chain (if
# any) never gets allocated. Discriminating: if NOT NULL were checked
# row-by-row in a single-pass loop, row 0 would be appended before
# row 1's failure aborted the statement. Tacked SELECT count(*)
# guards against silent corruption: a partial-insert regression
# would diverge from sqlite3's "<error>" output here.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(a NOT NULL, b);
QUERY: INSERT INTO t VALUES(1, 'first'), (NULL, 'second'); SELECT count(*) FROM t

# Cold-reopen carries the NOT NULL bit through sqlite_schema re-parse:
# the schema scanner re-runs `parseColumnDef` against the stored sql
# column at open time. SETUP creates+populates via sqlite3; sqlite0
# opens the file (running loadFromPager → parseColumnDef → not_null
# bits restored) and the rejecting INSERT must still trip.
SETUP: CREATE TABLE t(a TEXT NOT NULL, b INTEGER); INSERT INTO t VALUES('hi', 1);
QUERY: INSERT INTO t(b) VALUES(99)
